course_description:
This course examines the ways in which humanities traditions are embedded in particular cultural traditions. It has a particular interest on those traditions that challenge conceptions of the role, function, and aims of study of the Humanities, especially within the West. This includes a strong focus on the contribution that Indigenous peoples have made to critiquing and developing Humanities scholarship and providing alternative models of inquiry. It examines the conditions that lead to cross-cultural exchange.

Welcome to HUMN2200: Cross-Cultural Humanities, where we will explore the role of cross-cultural communication in the formation of modern culture, and humanities disciplines. We will approach this topic from three perspectives: 'Our Heroes & Origin Narratives', 'Boundaries of Humanity/Humanities', and 'Cross-Cultural Exchange'. You will be required to attend a weekly 1.5-hr lecture, participate in a 1.5hr-tutorial,ﾠand read a short text (or set of texts, films and/or art works).The tutorials will focus on discussion of these texts, and matters introduced in the weekly lectures, alongside means of making use of your humanities skills in your future social interactions and work life.The assessments for the course will be formative and authentic, focusing on articulating your own place within modern humanities research, and contributing to the Advanced Humanities YouTube channel.

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:
restrictions: Restricted to students enrolled in the BAdvHum(Hons) and BHum/LLB(Hons)

course_aims:
This course examines the ways in which humanities traditions are embedded in particular cultural traditions. It has a particular interest on those traditions that challenge conceptions of the role, function, and aims of study of the Humanities, especially within the West. This includes a strong focus on the contribution that Indigenous peoples have made to critiquing and developing Humanities scholarship and providing alternative models of inquiry. Course aims:

learning_outcomes.description: Draw on a range of interdisciplinary skills to identify, critique and understand socio-cultural issues.
Demonstrate integration of prior and current scholarship into a cohesive interdisciplinary context.
Appreciate the breadth and cross-cultural nature of the problems to which humanities research offers potential advanced and alternative thinking.
Discuss socio-cultural problems with different audiences at appropriate levels.
Conceptualise the skills pertinent to humanities interdisciplinary research.
Critically reflect on your own position in society, and the future work market.

assessment_summary.title: Video for Advanced Humanities Youtube Channel

assessment_details.title: Seminar Participation
assessment_details.task_description: Participation is essential for passing this course, and requires you to interact respectfully and learn from your peers. Students are expected to participate in seminar discussions and relevant seminar tasks. This assessment requires you to show evidence of preparation for seminars and participate in large and small group discussions with relevant and well-focused comments. Interaction in lectures and on Padlet is not essential, but will earn you additional points. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Students who miss classes may complete alternative assessments by request from the course coordinator.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Storying Yourself
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: In this short reflective text (500-750 words) , each student will position themselves (their social, cultural and academic background) in relation to one of the texts and/or origin stories discussed in weeks 2-4, and construct an argument about its place in modern culture and/or humanities discourse. This is an IN-CLASS closed-book assessment , that should be submitted under examination conditions in your tutorials in week 5. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit in class.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Students who miss their assessment due to medical reasons or exceptional circumstances may complete an alternative writing session by request from the course coordinator. Due to the nature of this assessment piece, all extensions should be negotiated with the course coordinator.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Video for Advanced Humanities Youtube Channel
assessment_details.task_description: Each student will submit a short video or podcast presentation (7-10 minutes, which is about 1,000 words) on a focused topic within the general research topic area 'Boundaries of Humanity/Humanities' A SELECTION OF EXCEPTIONALLY GOOD VIDEOS/PODCASTS WILL BE POSTED ON THE ADVANCED HUMANITIES YOUTUBE CHANNEL This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Critical Reflection on Practical AI
assessment_details.other_conditions: Work integrated learning. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: In this critical reflection, you will be required to use an Artificial Intelligence (AI) model, ChatGPT, to generate an employment application package (including a CV and cover letter) for a graduate position within a chosen industry (see options in section A below). You will then critically reflect on the presence/absence of cross-cultural nuance and diversity within the AI-generated material, considering it from the perspective of your chosen industry, one or two specific perspectives (see options in section B below) and reading material studied in the course. In doing so, you are required to reflect on your own cultural background and influences, demonstrate an understanding of cultural complexity, and identify cultural influences that inform your response. This critical reflection ( ca 1,500 words ) should also refer to appropriate secondary reading material. These resources could include required or recommended readings selected from the HUMN2200 course profile, alongside cultural studies research material and non-academic commentary on themes such as disciplinarity, diversity, and the cultural impact of AI in industry. Your application package should focus on ONE industry from Section A, and your critical reflection should consider it from ONE or TWO perspectives in Section B : SECTION A): 1. Social Work and/or Youth Counselling 2. Judiciary and Corrections 3. Education – Primary & Secondary 4. Education – Tertiary & Research 5. Government or Public Service 6. Libraries, Galleries or Arts Institutions SECTION B): 1. Gender Identity 2. Age 3. Education 4. Cultural Identity 5. Religion 6. Embodiment Your submission should include BOTH your AI-generated employment application package and your critical reflection. Your application package is not included in the word count. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources:
Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.
Own copy required
You'll need to have your own copy of the following reading resources. We've indicated below if you need a personal copy of the reading materials or your own item.
Item Description Book The Fairy Tales of Charles Perrault by Carter; Angela - 2005 Publisher: Penguin Books Australia ISBN: 9780143105367

learning_activities.topic: Week 1: Introduction to Cross-Cultural Engagement Introduction to the Course. Lecturer: Dr Karin Sellberg Required Reading: no preparation required. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Week 2: Our Heroes & Origin Narratives, part 1 The Afterlife of European Fairytales Lecturer: Dr Karin Sellberg Guest Interview: Dr Tamlyn Avery Required Reading: Selection of Charles Perault/Brothers Grimm Fairy Tales Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Week 3: Our Heroes & Origin Narratives, part 2 The Cross-Cultural Journey of the Gilgamesh Epos Guest Lecturer: Professor Alastair Blanshard Required Reading: The Gilgamesh Epos Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Week 4: Our Heroes & Origin Narratives, part 3 Local 'Dreamtime' Stories and Knowledge Practices Lecturer: Dr Karin Sellberg Guest Interview: Professor Tracey Bunda Required Reading: Selection of Dreamtime Stories Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Week 5: Practical Narratives & Storying Indigenous methodologies of 'Storying', and the difference between different story-telling modes. Tutorials this week will be dedicated to your first IN-CLASS Assessment: Storying Yourself Lecturer: Dr Karin Sellberg Guest Interview: Professor Tracey Bunda Required Reading: Selection from Louise Gwenneth Phillips, Tracey Bunda, Elizabeth P. Quintero, 'Research Through, With and As Storying' (2018) Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Week 6: Boundaries of Humanity/Humanities, part 1 Exchange between Human and Animal, Past and Present, East and West Lecturer: Dr Karin Sellberg Required Reading: Angela Carter, The Company of Wolves & Other Stories (1978 Short Stories) & Fireworks (1988 Short Stories) Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Week 7: Boundaries of Humanity/Humanities, part 2 The boundaries between Christianity and Native American knowledge traditions Guest Lecturer: Dr James Lancaster Required Reading: Thomas King, selection of short stories from 'One Good Story, That One: Stories' (1993) Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Week 8: Boundaries of Humanity/Humanities, part 3 This week we will discuss the intersection between art, science and embodiment. Tutorials this week will be co-facilitated with the UQ Art Museum. Lecturer: Dr Karin Sellberg Required Reading: Arnold I. Davidson, 'The Horror of Monsters' (1991) Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Week 9: Boundaries of Humanity/Humanities, part 4 The Mind/Body Divide and Digital/Biological Life Lecturer: Dr Karin Sellberg Guest Interview: Dr Lucy Fraser Required Watching: Mamoru Oshii, 'Ghost in the Shell' (1995 Movie) Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Week 10: Cross-Cultural Exchange, part 1 The Boundaries Between Humans and the Environment. This week's lecture will be recorded due to the Labour Day public holiday. Lecturer: Dr Karin Sellberg Required Reading: Selection from Anna Tsing, 'The Mushroom at the End of the World' Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Week 11: Cross-Cultural Exchange, part 2 Borderlands and Border Work in the context of Native American hybrid identities Lecturers: Dr Karin Sellberg and Dr Cassandra Byrnes Required Reading: Selection from Gloria Anzaldua, 'Borderlands/La Frontera: The New Mestiza' Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Week 12: Cross-Cultural Exchange, Part 3 'Chinatown' and the Modern Hybrid Society in Culture and Art Lecturer: Dr Karin Sellberg Guest Interview: Professor Ien Ang Required Reading: Ien Ang, 'Navigating complexity: From cultural critique to cultural intelligence' (2011); Selection from Ien Ang et. al., 'Chinatown Unbound' (2019) Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Week 13: Cross-Cultural Exchange, part 4 Positioning Yourself in Future Society and on the Future Work Market. Lecturer: Dr Karin Sellberg Required Reading: Jenny Nguyễn, 'Am I Employable? The Perspective of a History Undergraduate Student in Australia' (2021); 'The path to prosperity: Why the future of work is human' (2023 Deloitte Report) Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
